message.content:
## Synthesis: research report

**Question:** DESIGNING AND TRAINING A VOICE / TTS MODEL TO BE MORE COMPREHENSIBLE AT HIGH-SPEED LISTENING. Deliver BOTH the underlying science AND concrete recommendations for a local open-source TTS podcast pipeline (currently uses Kokoro TTS). Cover these 6 angles: (1) ACOUSTIC/PROSODIC PROPERTIES that survive time-compression / make a voice intelligible when sped up — "clear speech"/hyperarticulation, vowel-space expansion, consonant emphasis/sharper consonant-vowel transitions, modulation-spectrum shape, F0/pitch characteristics, and pause/rhythm structure. (2) FORMANT SYNTHESIS vs NEURAL TTS at extreme speed — why blind screen-reader power users overwhelmingly prefer old formant synthesizers (Eloquence, DECtalk, eSpeak-NG) over natural neural voices at 18-22 syllables/second; consistency, crispness, latency, predictability; quantify the naturalness-vs-intelligibility trade-off. (3) RATE-AWARE / CONTROLLABLE TTS GENERATION — conditioning or fine-tuning a neural TTS to NATIVELY generate fast-but-clear speech (speaking-rate control, prosody control, training on clear-speech/Lombard-speech corpora) vs. post-hoc time-scale modification; CRITICAL: how much MEASURABLE comprehensibility gain does native fast generation or fine-tuning actually buy over simply time-compressing a normal voice? (the user will only invest in fine-tuning/training if the gain is significant). (4) DSP ENHANCEMENT for intelligibility at speed — formant/clarity enhancement, spectral shaping + dynamic-range compression, consonant boosting, content-aware PER-PHONEME variable-rate compression (compress steady-state vowels more, preserve consonant transitions), and modulation-envelope enhancement; these apply to ANY engine's output. (5) CONCRETE ENGINES/VOICES & CONFIG KNOBS — head-to-head comparison of which TTS engines/voices are most intelligible at high speed (Kokoro, Piper, eSpeak-NG/Eloquence, RHVoice, Azure/Google neural, ElevenLabs) and the tunable settings (speaking rate, pitch, pause length) that improve speed-comprehensibility. (6) HOW TO MEASURE "comprehensibility at speed" — objective intelligibility metrics (STOI, STMI/spectro-temporal modulation index, modulation-spectrum analysis) and practical subjective A/B / MUSHRA listening-test protocols, so one can decide whether a fine-tune cleared a "significant gain" bar before committing. CONSTRAINTS/CONTEXT: pipeline runs LOCALLY/on-device (open-source Kokoro), so favor approaches compatible with local inference. Output should serve TWO use cases: a normal natural-sounding default voice for general students, AND an optional aggressively speed-optimized track for trained power listeners (robotic is acceptable for that track if measurably clearer). For EACH effort tier (pick/configure a voice < DSP post-processing < fine-tune an open model < train from scratch), state the expected comprehensibility gain, the cost/effort, and whether it is worth it. Prefer peer-reviewed and authoritative sources; include practitioner/community knowledge from the blind/screen-reader speed-listening community where relevant.

22 claims survived 3-vote adversarial verification. Merge semantic duplicates and synthesize.

## Confirmed claims
### [0] When time-compressed to the same fast rate, normal speech is MORE intelligible than clear (hyperarticulated) speech — meaning the intelligibility advantage of clear-speech corpora does not survive aggressive time-compression and can even invert. This directly bears on whether training on clear-speech corpora is worthwhile for a fast-listening pipeline.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://arxiv.org/abs/1901.07239 (primary)
Quote: "Even when controlling for duration, compressed normal speech is still more intelligible than compressed clear speech."
Verifier evidence (high): The claim is well-supported by the primary source and corroborated independently. The source is arXiv 1901.07239, "Non linear time compression of clear and normal speech at high rates" by Valentini-Botinhao, Wester, Yamagishi, Toman, Pucher & Schabus (Centre for Speech Technology Research, Univ. of Edinburgh — established speech/TTS researchers; not marketing or a forum). WebFetch of the abstract and multiple web searches confirm the supporting quote verbatim: "compressed normal speech is more intelligible than compressed clear speech even when the overall duration of speech and silence is kept the same across styles." This directly supports both the core claim (normal > clear under aggressive time-compression) and the duration-controlled framing. The "inversion" framing is also corroborated: a search result states "Unprocessed fast speech was less intelligible than linearly compressed clear speech at moderate rates, but at the highest speaking rates clear speech was worse than fast speech" — i.e., the advantage holds at moderate rates and inverts only at the highest compression rates. IMPORTANT NUANCE (not a refutation): Krause & Braida (2002, JASA, PubMed 12430828) find that NATURALLY PRODUCED clear speech retains its advantage even at faster natural speaking rates ("clear speech has inherent acoustic properties that contribute to high intelligibility without altering rate"). This is a different mechanism (natural fast production) from signal-domain time-compression of pre-recorded slow clear speech, which is exactly the claim's scope, so it delineates rather than contradicts the claim. The finding is a stable psychoacoustic result, not a fast-moving benchmark, so the 2019 date is not a staleness concern. Two practical caveats on STRENGTH: (a) the inversion is specific to AGGRESSIVE/high compression rates, not all fast rates; (b) this is one study, so "can even invert" is appropriately hedged. The claim as worded ("does not survive aggressive time-compression and CAN even invert") matches the evidence's strength.

### [1] Natively-recorded fast speech is LESS intelligible than a normal-rate voice that is linearly time-compressed to the same rate, suggesting post-hoc time-scale modification of a normal voice can beat naturally-produced fast speech for intelligibility — relevant to the native-fast-generation vs. time-compression trade-off (angle 3).
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://arxiv.org/abs/1901.07239 (primary)
Quote: "unprocessed fast speech was less intelligible than linearly compressed normal speech"
Verifier evidence (high): The claim is directly and verbatim supported by the primary source. arXiv:1901.07239 (a preprint of work in Speech Communication on time-compressed speech) states in its abstract: "unprocessed fast speech was less intelligible than linearly compressed normal speech." This is exactly the claim. The finding is corroborated by multiple independent peer-reviewed studies: (a) Janse et al., "Word perception in fast speech: artificially time-compressed vs. naturally produced fast speech" (Speech Communication, ScienceDirect S0167639303001092) found natural fast speech is less intelligible, partly due to reduced vowel space and uneven (non-linear) segment shortening; (b) the 2016 Springer perception study (10.3758/s13414-016-1067-x) found natural fast speech is perceived as faster and harder to process than linearly compressed speech; transfer of perceptual adaptation only went from compressed to natural, not vice versa. CRUCIALLY for the TTS/synthetic application domain, the study "Intelligibility of time-compressed synthetic speech: Compression method and speaking style" (Speech Communication, ScienceDirect S0167639315000977) found that "generating speech at a normal speaking rate and then applying linear compression resulted in the most intelligible speech at all tested rates" for English voices — directly extending the claim to synthetic/TTS speech and supporting the time-compression-beats-native-fast conclusion. No contradicting credible source was found. QUALIFICATIONS (do not refute, but bound the claim): (1) The arXiv finding concerns NATURAL human speech, not neural-TTS native-fast generation; the claim correctly hedges ("relevant to," "suggesting") rather than over-asserting TTS evidence. (2) The synthetic study adds a talker-dependent nuance: a skilled German speaker who articulated cleanly at fast rate (few deletions/substitutions) yielded intelligible results when his fast data was compressed, meaning the advantage of "normal-then-compress" is not absolute but depends on how cleanly the fast version is articulated. Source quality (peer-reviewed Speech Communication / Springer), recency-stability (psychoacoustics, not a fast-moving benchmark), and convergent multi-study support all match the claim's strength. Sources: https://arxiv.org/abs/1901.07239 ; https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0167639315000977 ; https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0167639303001092 ; https://link.springer.com/article/10.3758/s13414-016-1067-x

### [2] Greater dynamic formant movement (linear movement between vowel nucleus endpoints, VL) predicts higher vowel intelligibility, whereas non-linear formant movement within the nucleus does not — meaning preserving formant transitions/movement is what carries vowel identity, not just static targets.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4048446/ (primary)
Quote: "Greater linear formant movement between the endpoints of the vowel nucleus (VL) was associated with higher vowel intelligibility, while non-linear movement within the nucleus (TLadj) was unrelated to vowel intelligibility"
Verifier evidence (high): The primary source PMC4048446 (Neel et al., "Acoustic correlates of vowel intelligibility in clear and conversational speech for young normal-hearing and elderly hearing-impaired listeners," peer-reviewed, PubMed 24907820, 2014) directly and verbatim supports the claim. WebFetch confirmed the GLMM results: VL (linear formant movement between 20%-80% nucleus endpoints in F1xF2 space) beta=0.576, p<0.0001 (highly significant positive effect on intelligibility); TLadj (non-linear deviation of the trajectory from the straight-line vector VL) beta=-0.032, n.s. (unrelated to intelligibility). Study sample: 41 talkers, 7 young normal-hearing + 40 elderly hearing-impaired listeners, 10 English vowels in /bVd/ context, clear vs conversational speech. The finding held for BOTH listener groups (VL effect slightly stronger for young NH, marginal group interaction p=0.0684 -- a nuance, not a contradiction). The interpretive extension ('preserving formant transitions/movement carries vowel identity, not just static targets') is independently corroborated by the broader Vowel Inherent Spectral Change (VISC) literature (Springer 'Vowel Inherent Spectral Change' 2013; JASA studies on VISC): dynamic-change models classify vowel categories far more accurately than steady-state models, and flat-formant (static) vowels show large identification-accuracy drops. No contradicting source found in two targeted searches. Source quality (peer-reviewed JASA-tier primary study with proper mixed-effects analysis) matches the claim's strength; the topic is foundational acoustic phonetics, not a fast-moving field, so 2014 publication date does not make it outdated.</parameter>
</invoke>


### [3] Longer vowel duration improves intelligibility for both listener groups but especially for elderly hearing-impaired listeners, by giving more processing time; clear speech lengthened vowels by a factor of ~1.5 (290 ms vs 204 ms).
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4048446/ (primary)
Quote: "Longer vowel duration helped both groups but especially EHI listeners, perhaps by allowing more processing time for vowel identifications."
Verifier evidence (high): VERIFIED against the primary source (Ferguson & Quené, "Acoustic correlates of vowel intelligibility in clear and conversational speech for young normal-hearing and elderly hearing-impaired listeners," J. Acoust. Soc. Am. 135(6):3570-3584, 2014 — peer-reviewed, PMC4048446). Every element of the claim matches the paper verbatim: (1) Listener groups = young normal-hearing (YNH, 19-30) vs elderly hearing-impaired (EHI, 65-87), matching "both groups... especially elderly hearing-impaired." (2) Mechanism: paper states "Longer vowel duration helped both groups but especially EHI listeners, perhaps by allowing more processing time for vowel identifications" — exact match including "more processing time." (3) Numbers: paper reports "average vowel durations of 290 ms in clear speech and 204 ms in conversational speech" and "vowel duration increased in clear speech by a factor of 1.5" — exact match to "~1.5 (290 ms vs 204 ms)." Source quality (JASA, top-tier acoustics journal) exceeds the bar for this claim. Not outdated: vowel lengthening in clear speech is a foundational, decades-replicated finding in speech acoustics (cf. Picheny et al. 1986), not a fast-moving field. Not marketing/cherry-picked — empirical study. One minor unstated nuance: the paper notes the per-vowel duration effect on identification odds was significant "only for tense vowels," which qualifies but does not contradict the directional claim (and the claim's own "perhaps"/"by giving more processing time" mirrors the paper's hedged phrasing).

### [4] Clear speech expands the vowel space: front-vowel F2 increases and back-vowel F2 decreases, with F2 range expanding ~14% and F1 range ~9% — quantifying the vowel-space-expansion / hyperarticulation effect that aids intelligibility.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4048446/ (primary)
Quote: "The F2 range expanded by an average of 14% (0.38 Bark increase), with "F1 range expansion of approximately 9% (0.23 Bark).""
Verifier evidence (high): The primary source (Ferguson & Quené, PMC4048446, "Acoustic correlates of vowel intelligibility in clear and conversational speech for young normal-hearing and elderly hearing-impaired listeners") verbatim confirms every number in the claim. F2: "The clear/conversational ratio for F2 range was 1.14 on average [...] the 14% expansion corresponds with an average F2 range increase of 0.38 Bark]". F1: "ratio for F1 range was 1.09 on average [...] the 9% expansion corresponds with an average increase of the F1 range by 0.23 Bark]". Both significant (F2: t(40)=5.6, p<0.0001; F1: t(40)=4.3, p=0.0001). The directional pattern is also confirmed: "the front vowels /i/, /ɪ/, /e/, /ε/, /æ/ showed significantly higher F2 values in clear speech, while the back vowels /o/, /ʊ/, /u/ had significantly lower F2 values." Source quality is high (peer-reviewed JASA-class study, NIH PMC). Only nuance: the "aids intelligibility" framing is association, not universal causation — this paper found expansion improved vowel intelligibility for young normal-hearing listeners but NOT for elderly hearing-impaired listeners. That qualification does not refute the claim, which targets the general-student/podcast (normal-hearing) use case and is correctly characterized as quantifying the hyperarticulation effect.

### [5] Clear speech (deliberate hyperarticulation) produces a measurable intelligibility advantage over conversational speech for consonants/fricatives, with intelligibility benefits in prior literature ranging from 7 to 38 percentage points; in this study clear speech significantly lowered SNR identification thresholds for both normal-hearing and simulated-hearing-impaired listeners.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://www.isca-archive.org/interspeech_2007/maniwa07_interspeech.pdf (primary)
Quote: "Clear speech elicited in laboratory settings has been shown to benefit a variety of listener populations under different adverse conditions (e.g. noise, reverberation) [e.g. 3, 4, 5], resulting in intelligibility advantages relative to “conversational” speech ranging from 7 to 38 percentage points."
Verifier evidence (high): VERIFIED against primary source. Extracted the exact text from the Maniwa, Jongman & Wade (2007) Interspeech PDF (isca-archive.org). The verbatim sentence reads: "Clear speech elicited in laboratory settings has been shown to benefit a variety of listener populations under different adverse conditions (e.g. noise, reverberation) [e.g. 3, 4, 5], resulting in intelligibility advantages relative to 'conversational' speech ranging from 7 to 38 percentage points." This matches the claim's '7 to 38 percentage points' figure exactly. The SNR-threshold finding is independently confirmed by the peer-reviewed JASA publication of the same study (Maniwa, Jongman & Wade 2008, J. Acoust. Soc. Am. 123(2):1114-1125, PubMed 18247912): "Two experiments measured babble signal-to-noise ratio thresholds for fricative minimal pair distinctions for 14 normal-hearing listeners and 14 listeners with simulated sloping, recruiting impairment. Results indicated that clear speech helped both groups overall." The study is explicitly about fricative consonants. MINOR QUALIFICATION (not a refutation): the abstract notes "for impaired listeners, reliable clear speech intelligibility advantages were not found for non-sibilant pairs" — i.e., the benefit for impaired listeners was overall but not universal across all fricative pairs. The claim does not assert universality, so this is a nuance rather than a contradiction. Source quality (ISCA peer-reviewed conference + JASA journal) matches claim strength; age (2007/2008) is fine for foundational acoustic-phonetics science. Note: also independently observed that the SAME paper immediately adds a robustness caveat ("some results call into question the robustness of the 'clear speech' effect... negative clear-speech benefits in elderly hearing-impaired listeners"), but this does not undermine the specific claim under review.

### [6] For normal-hearing listeners, the dominant acoustic modification driving the clear-speech intelligibility gain in fricatives is a shift of spectral energy toward higher frequency regions, evidenced by significant positive correlations between intelligibility advantage and DFT/LPC spectral-peak location and spectral moment 1 (M1).
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://www.isca-archive.org/interspeech_2007/maniwa07_interspeech.pdf (primary)
Quote: "Significant positive correlations (p<0.05) were obtained between intelligibility advantages and acoustic modifications in DFT and LPC peak location and spectral moment 1 (M1)... These results suggest that a shift of spectral energy to higher frequency regions in clear fricatives is most closely related to the overall intelligibility enhancement."
Verifier evidence (high): Verified the supporting quote verbatim by extracting raw text directly from the primary PDF (cached at the local tool-results path), not relying on WebFetch summary. Exact text in the paper: "Significant positive correlations (p<0.05) were obtained between intelligibility advantages and acoustic modifications in DFT and LPC peak location and spectral moment 1 (M1), whereas significant negative correlations were found for M3. These results suggest that a shift of spectral energy to higher frequency regions in clear fricatives is most closely related to the overall intelligibility enhancement." The abstract scopes this precisely to the right population: "a shift of energy concentration toward higher frequency regions and greater source strength contributed to the clear speech effect for normal-hearing listeners, while listeners with simulated loss seemed to benefit mostly from cues involving lower frequency regions." The claim correctly limits itself to NORMAL-HEARING listeners and does NOT overgeneralize (the paper found the opposite direction for simulated hearing-impaired listeners), so it avoids the obvious refutation. Source is peer-reviewed (Maniwa, Jongman & Wade, Interspeech 2007), authored by established speech-perception researchers, and independently corroborated by the companion peer-reviewed paper Maniwa, Jongman & Wade (2008), J. Acoust. Soc. Am. 123(2):1114 ("Perception of clear fricatives by normal-hearing and simulated hearing-impaired listeners"). No contradicting source found in web search. The finding is a settled phonetics result (not a fast-moving field), so 2007 dating is not a weakness. Minor caveat (non-refuting): the word "dominant" paraphrases the paper's "most closely related"/"important predictors," and the paper also credits "greater source strength" alongside the frequency shift; the claim's correlational framing ("evidenced by significant positive correlations") matches the paper's correlational (non-causal) methodology.

### [7] The temporal modulations most important for speech intelligibility lie in the 1-7 Hz range, with spectral modulations below ~1 cycle/kHz being most important; the speech modulation transfer function is bandpass in the temporal domain and low-pass in the spectral domain.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://journals.plos.org/ploscompbiol/article?id=10.1371%2Fjournal.pcbi.1000302 (primary)
Quote: "temporal modulations from 1 to 7 Hz and spectral modulations <1 cycles/kHz were the most important ... the MTF was bandpass in temporal modulations and low-pass in spectral modulations"
Verifier evidence (high): The claim is verbatim supported by the primary source. Elliott & Theunissen (2009), "The Modulation Transfer Function for Speech Intelligibility," PLoS Computational Biology 5(3):e1000302, PMID 19266016 (UC Berkeley, Helen Wills Neuroscience Institute) states in its abstract: "the MTF was bandpass in temporal modulations and low-pass in spectral modulations: temporal modulations from 1 to 7 Hz and spectral modulations <1 cycles/kHz were the most important." This matches the supporting quote exactly. PubMed and the PMC full-text (PMC2639724) independently confirm identical wording. The paper additionally notes comprehension is significantly impaired when temporal modulations <12 Hz or spectral modulations <4 cycles/kHz are removed (a broader necessary band), which is consistent with — not contradictory to — the narrower "most important" core. Cross-checked against the wider literature: the consensus that intelligibility-critical envelope information sits in the low-frequency temporal band (roughly 1-16 Hz, peaking near the ~4 Hz syllabic rate; STI/Houtgast-Steeneken tradition, Drullman 1994, Greenberg) is fully compatible with the 1-7 Hz range. The only genuine debates in the literature concern the precise peak (4 Hz vs band) and the exact upper edge, neither of which refutes the bandpass-temporal/low-pass-spectral characterization as stated. Source quality is high: peer-reviewed, foundational, highly cited primary research using a novel joint spectrotemporal modulation-filtering technique — not marketing, forum, or cherry-picked benchmark. The 2009 date reflects settled psychoacoustics, not a fast-moving field, so it is not outdated. No credible contradicting source was found.

### [8] There exists a core spectro-temporal modulation region below ~8 Hz temporally and ~4 cycles/kHz spectrally that is essential for speech comprehension; comprehension is significantly impaired when modulations below these cutoffs are removed.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://journals.plos.org/ploscompbiol/article?id=10.1371%2Fjournal.pcbi.1000302 (primary)
Quote: "there exists a region, or 'core', of modulations below ∼4 cycles/kHz and ∼8 Hz that are essential for comprehension"
Verifier evidence (high): The claim is verbatim-supported by the primary source. Elliott & Theunissen (2009), "The Modulation Transfer Function for Speech Intelligibility," PLOS Computational Biology (DOI: 10.1371/journal.pcbi.1000302), states in its conclusion: "the results from the spectral and temporal domains suggested that there exists a region, or 'core', of modulations below ∼4 cycles/kHz and ∼8 Hz that are essential for comprehension." The abstract independently confirms the impairment direction and significance: "Comprehension was significantly impaired when temporal modulations <12 Hz or spectral modulations <4 cycles/kHz were removed." Checklist results: (1) Quote-support — exact match; the claim restates the paper's own integrated "core" cutoffs (~8 Hz, ~4 cycles/kHz) and the significant-impairment finding, no overreach. The ~8 Hz figure is the paper's own synthesized core boundary (the 12 Hz is the initial low-pass impairment onset; the paper itself collapses these to ~8 Hz). (2) No contradicting evidence found; the result is mainstream and consistent with the broader MTF/STI literature (syllabic rate ~4-7 Hz). (3) Source quality high — primary, peer-reviewed, highly cited journal article, matching the claim's strength. (4) Not meaningfully outdated — this is foundational human-auditory psychophysics (filtering + transcription-in-noise listening tests), not a fast-moving engineering benchmark; the biology is unchanged. (5) Not marketing/forum/cherry-picked — rigorous academic psychophysics study. Verified via PMC mirror (PMC2639724) and PLOS original.

### [9] Inserting silent gaps ('repackaging') into time-compressed speech at theta-compatible rates restores intelligibility, with the optimal restoration ('rescue point') at ~5 packets/second for young adults (center of the 3-8 Hz theta range). This means rhythmic restructuring, not just raw rate, governs comprehensibility at speed.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6181647/ (primary)
Quote: "the rescue point for the young adults occurred at 5 packets per second, at the center of the typical theta frequency range for young adults (3 to 8 Hz), while the rescue point for the older adults occurred at 4 packets per second"
Verifier evidence (high): The claim is accurately supported by its primary source, PMC6181647 ("The possible role of brain rhythms in perceiving fast speech: Evidence from adult aging," Journal of the Acoustical Society of America, Oct 2018 — peer-reviewed primary research). The supporting quote is verbatim accurate: the paper defines the "rescue point" as the earliest tested rate at which repackaging (dividing time-compressed speech into packets delivered with inserted silent gaps) yielded significantly better digit-recognition than continuous time-compression — 5 packets/s for young adults (center of the 3-8 Hz theta range) and 4 packets/s for older adults. The methodology (digit recognition accuracy across 6-21 syll/s, repackaged at 2-7 packets/s) measures intelligibility directly, supporting that this is a comprehensibility finding. The core conceptual claim — that rhythmic restructuring, not raw rate alone, governs intelligibility — is the established finding of the Ghitza/Greenberg line of work: repackaged speech at the same information rate is markedly more intelligible than continuously compressed speech (poor ~50% WER without gaps, restored with 20-120 ms gaps). No contradicting source was found. The one qualifying paper (Nature Scientific Reports 2023, s41598-023-29755-x) is complementary, not contradicting: it argues the benefit may ALSO involve temporal-fine-structure processing in addition to theta synchronization — qualifying the causal MECHANISM but not the behavioral rescue-point finding. The only soft caveat is that theta-oscillation CAUSATION is presented as the TEMPO hypothesis/correlational framework rather than proven causation, but the claim hedges appropriately ("theta-compatible rates") and its load-bearing facts (rescue point = 5/s for young adults; rhythm > raw rate) are directly and correctly sourced.

### [10] The hyperarticulation benefit to comprehension persisted across multiple adverse-noise levels (quiet, +16 dB, +12 dB, +8 dB SNR), demonstrating that clear-speech intelligibility gains survive signal degradation.
Vote: 2-0 · Source: https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00426-021-01595-2 (primary)
Quote: "This benefit persisted across noise levels (quiet, +16 dB, +12 dB, +8 dB SNR), with no significant performance differences between native L1 speakers and both early and late L2 learners."
Verifier evidence (high): VERIFIED via open-access PMC full text (PMC9177484) and Springer abstract: Kangatharan, Uther & Gobet (2022), "The effect of hyperarticulation on speech comprehension under adverse listening conditions," Psychological Research 86(5):1535-1546 — a peer-reviewed primary study. The study tested EXACTLY the four conditions in the claim: quiet, +16 dB, +12 dB, +8 dB SNR. Abstract verbatim: "Mean ratings showed all three groups found hyperarticulated speech samples easier to understand than non-hyperarticulated speech at all listening conditions." Three groups: 16 L1 British English, 16 early L2 Mandarin, 16 late L2 Mandarin. Results: "There was no significant effect on the listener group" — confirming the claim's secondary assertion of no L1/L2 difference. So the benefit persisting across all four noise levels AND the no-significant-L1/L2-difference are both directly supported by the source. Source quality matches claim strength (peer-reviewed, current 2021/2022, not marketing/forum). MINOR CAVEAT (not refutation-worthy): the dependent measure was a SUBJECTIVE 1-6 ease-of-understanding rating ("indicate on a scale from 1 to 6 ... 1 = not easy to understand at all; 6 = very easy"), i.e., perceived comprehensibility, NOT an objective word-recognition accuracy / % -correct intelligibility score. The claim's phrase "clear-speech intelligibility gains" slightly overstates by implying objective intelligibility; it should read "perceived comprehensibility." Also note the magnitude of the noise was modest (positive SNRs of +8 to +16 dB, not heavy degradation), so "survive signal degradation" is true but only across these mild-to-moderate adverse conditions. These are wording nuances, not contradictions — no credible source disputes the finding.

### [11] Blind people who train in ultra-fast speech comprehension can understand spoken language at up to ~22 syllables per second, roughly 2.5-3x the maximum rate of normal-sighted listeners (~8 syl/s).
Vote: 2-0 · Source: https://bmcneurosci.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/1471-2202-14-74 (primary)
Quote: "Individuals suffering from vision loss...may learn to understand spoken language at a rate of up to about 22 syllables (syl) per second - exceeding by far the maximum performance level of normal-sighted listeners (ca. 8 syl/s)."
Verifier evidence (high): The claim is faithfully and accurately supported by the cited primary source. Dietrich et al. (BMC Neuroscience 2013, DOI 10.1186/1471-2202-14-74; verified via open-access mirror PMC3847124) states verbatim that blind individuals "may learn to understand spoken language at a rate of up to about 22 syllables (syl) per second - exceeding by far the maximum performance level of normal-sighted listeners (ca. 8 syl/s)." The supporting quote matches the source exactly. The "2.5-3x" multiplier is correct arithmetic (22/8 = 2.75x). Source quality is appropriate: a peer-reviewed fMRI neuroscience study, corroborated by the same group's Frontiers paper (10.3389/fnhum.2013.00701) and widely cited as the canonical figure in this literature. The claim correctly frames 22 syl/s as an upper limit reached through training ("who train... up to ~22"), matching the source's framing (achieved via "repeated exposure to accelerated verbal utterances"), so it is not an overreach. The one genuine caveat: the source's "~8 syl/s" sighted maximum is a conservative simplification — other literature (e.g., time-compressed speech studies) puts sighted comprehension breakdown at ~11-16 syl/s, which would shrink the multiplier toward ~1.5x rather than 2.5-3x. However, that contradiction works AGAINST inflation, not for it, and the claim accurately reports what its cited source asserts. The finding is a stable psychophysical result (2013, not a fast-moving benchmark), not marketing or forum speculation. Core proposition is well-supported, current, and source-matched.

### [12] Trained blind (vision-impaired) listeners can learn to comprehend accelerated synthetic speech at rates up to approximately 22 syllables per second, far exceeding the ~8 syllables/second upper limit of normal sighted listeners.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3805979/ (primary)
Quote: "vision-impaired individuals may learn to understand accelerated spoken language at a rate of up to about 22 syllables (syl) per second (s) ... exceeding by far the capacities of normal sighted listeners whose upper limit is at ca. 8 syl/s"
Verifier evidence (high): The claim is a faithful paraphrase of the primary source and is independently corroborated. Primary source PMC3805979 (Dietrich, Hertrich & Ackermann, "Training of ultra-fast speech comprehension...", Frontiers in Human Neuroscience, 2013) states verbatim: "vision-impaired individuals may learn to understand accelerated spoken language at a rate of up to about 22 syllables (syl) per second (s)—exceeding by far the capacities of normal sighted listeners whose upper limit is at ca. 8 syl/s." A second peer-reviewed paper from the same group (PMC3847124, BMC Neuroscience, 2013) repeats the identical 22 vs 8 syl/s figures AND provides supporting experimental data: at the ultra-fast 16 syl/s condition, blind subjects averaged 59.7% comprehension (range 0-93%) vs 9.0% for sighted controls; at 8 syl/s both groups were comparable (84.1% blind vs 80.8% sighted). Scientific American also covered the work. The claim correctly uses "up to approximately 22" (the source frames 22 as a learnable upper ceiling, with experiments testing 16 syl/s). Source quality (multiple peer-reviewed neuroscience journals, Hertie Institute/Univ. Tübingen) matches the claim's strength. The finding concerns stable human cognitive/auditory capacity, so its 2013 date is not disqualifying. No contradicting source was found.

### [13] The ultra-fast speech-listening experiments with blind subjects used the Eloquence formant synthesizer (via the JAWS screen reader), the same class of formant synthesizer that blind screen-reader power users favor for high-speed listening.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3805979/ (primary)
Quote: "screen-reader JAWS (male voice, synthesizer "Eloquence,""
Verifier evidence (high): Both components of the claim are verified. (1) PRIMARY SOURCE (PMC3805979) confirms the study used Eloquence via JAWS with blind subjects at ultra-fast rates. The paper's text states "the screen-reader JAWS (male voice, synthesizer 'Eloquence,'" and elsewhere "formant synthesizer 'eloquence'." The study tested 8, 13, and 18 syllables/second and used five blind subjects (plus one sighted control). So Eloquence IS explicitly characterized as a formant synthesizer by the paper itself, and the supporting quote is accurate (not a misread). (2) The second clause — that this is "the same class of formant synthesizer that blind screen-reader power users favor for high-speed listening" — is independently corroborated: community/authoritative sources describe Eloquence as "the most popular speech synthesizer... understood at a high rate of speech," shipping with JAWS by default; and note that "Klatt synthesis... well-known synthesizers like DECtalk and Eloquence... are well suited for use by the blind because they are responsive, predictable." Minor technical nuance: Eloquence is more precisely a hybrid formant/concatenation system (rule-based formant synthesis for vowels/sonorants, prerecorded noise for stops/fricatives), but it is correctly classed as formant-based and the paper itself labels it a "formant synthesizer," so the claim is not an overreach. No contradicting evidence found; source is a peer-reviewed primary study; not outdated or marketing.

### [14] There is a divide between novice and expert screen-reader users in voice preference: novices prioritize voice naturalness while experts care little about TTS quality and instead prioritize reliability and application support.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3955012 (primary)
Quote: "Advanced users of screen readers cited little interest in TTS quality and instead voiced much more concern about TTS reliability and application support."
Verifier evidence (high): The primary source (PMC3955012, "The 'voice' has it: screen reader adoption and switching behavior among vision impaired persons in India") contains the verbatim supporting quote AND strengthens the claim: "Advanced users of screen readers cited little interest in TTS quality and instead voiced much more concern about TTS reliability and application support, as opposed to novice users who almost unanimously cited voice as the factor of preference for screen reader." Both halves of the claim are explicitly stated. It is a peer-reviewed study (n=146 survey + 20 interviews) with quantitative backing: the expert/novice difference in valuing application support was statistically significant (Chi-square=23.77, p<.001). The mechanism is documented (experts speed up audio, which degrades naturalness, so they stop caring about it). Independent corroboration from current blind/accessibility community sources (AFB Speech Synthesizers page, Fable, NVDA community) confirms power users favor responsiveness/predictability over naturalness and that natural voices are "hard to understand at a higher rate of speech." No contradicting source found. CAVEAT: the study is ~2014, India-context, and predates modern neural TTS (Eloquence vs eSpeak era), so the specific engines are dated — but the directional preference divide is independently confirmed by contemporary practitioner knowledge, so the claim's substance holds. Not an overreach, not contradicted, not marketing.

### [15] Expert users routinely speed up TTS output, which makes the voice sound more artificial/robotic, the very quality novices dislike, indicating that at high speed naturalness is sacrificed and is no longer the priority for trained listeners.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3955012 (primary)
Quote: "expert users typically speed up the audio output which causes the output to sound more artificial, precisely the quality that novice users tended to dislike about eSpeak"
Verifier evidence (high): VERIFIED against primary source PMC3955012 ("The 'voice' has it: screen reader adoption and switching behavior among vision impaired persons in India", peer-reviewed mixed-methods study). The supporting quote is verbatim-accurate. The paper's stated "critical finding": "the preference for a 'natural-sounding TTS voice' decreases as users became experts, since advanced users typically speed up the audio output which causes the output to sound more artificial, precisely the quality that novice users tended to dislike about eSpeak." The paper also documents that playback speed rises monotonically from novice to expert (default -> 300-500+ WPM) and that "the importance of the voice quality may take a backseat to the desire or need for speed." The claim faithfully restates this directional logic (speeding degrades naturalness; experts de-prioritize naturalness) without overreach. Independent searches found CORROBORATION (multiple sources echoing the intelligibility-over-naturalness trade-off for experienced blind listeners) and NO credible contradiction. Caveat: study context is ~2014 India, but the claim describes a stable perceptual/behavioral phenomenon (sped-up speech sounds more artificial; trained listeners tolerate it), not a fast-moving technical benchmark, so age does not invalidate it. Source quality matches the claim's moderate strength.

### [16] Recognition scores for time-compressed (TC) speech systematically over-estimate recognition of naturally-produced fast-rate speech; listeners do substantially worse on natural fast speech than on time-compressed speech at a comparable rate, so a TC voice does not behave like genuinely fast speech.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6377055/ (primary)
Quote: "Recognition of time-compressed sentences over-estimated recognition of natural fast sentences for both groups, especially for older listeners."
Verifier evidence (high): The claim is faithfully supported by the primary source: Gordon-Salant, Zion & Espy-Wilson (2014), "Recognition of time-compressed speech does not predict recognition of natural fast-rate speech by older listeners," J. Acoust. Soc. Am. (PMC6377055). The supporting quote is accurate and the article title itself asserts TC does not predict natural-fast recognition. Method: a talker recorded sentences at natural-fast rate (~40% faster); PSOLA in Praat time-compressed normal-rate recordings to match that duration. Result: recognition was significantly poorer for natural fast speech than for rate-matched TC speech (p<0.001) across quiet and noise, high- and low-context sentences; for both younger (n=13, 19-22yo) and older (n=12, 66-76yo) listeners, ALL individual data points except one sat above the linear-prediction line, i.e., TC consistently overestimated natural-fast recognition (effect larger for older listeners). Mechanism attributed to segmental distortions in natural fast speech: shorter phoneme durations plus deletions/distortions of stops and affricates from coarticulation and lenition (target undershoot) — distortions linear TC does not introduce. Independent corroboration from WebSearch (Janse 2004 non-uniform vs uniform compression; ScienceDirect work) shows generating/mimicking natural fast timing is LESS intelligible than linear compression of normal-rate speech, especially at ultra-fast rates — reinforcing, not refuting. Source is primary, peer-reviewed, top-tier (JASA), and the underlying speech-production science is durable (not a fast-moving-tech claim). No credible contradicting source found.

### [17] The intelligibility ranking from best to worst is normal-rate, then 40% time-compressed, then natural fast speech (statistically significant, p <= 0.006), establishing that uniform time-compression is more intelligible than naturally fast speech at matched rate.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6377055/ (primary)
Quote: "performance was best for the normal rate speech, significantly poorer for the 40TC rate, and poorest with natural fast speech"
Verifier evidence (high): The claim is well-supported by the primary source (PMC6377055, Gordon-Salant et al.) and corroborated by independent peer-reviewed literature. The source explicitly states: "each pairwise comparison among the three rates in both contexts was significant (p <= 0.006) except for HP normal rate vs 40TC" and that "performance was best for the normal rate speech, significantly poorer for the 40TC rate, and poorest with natural fast speech." Critically, the rate-matching is valid: the natural fast speech and 40TC speech were duration-matched (both ~0.6 fast-to-normal ratio); the talker's natural fast sentences "approximated 40% time-compression (fast-to-normal ratio = 0.6)," so 40% was chosen for the TC condition to match. Thus the comparison is genuinely at matched rate, supporting the claim that uniform time-compression is more intelligible than naturally fast speech. This is NOT an isolated result: it converges with Janse (2003, "Word perception in fast speech: artificially time-compressed vs. naturally produced fast speech," Speech Communication, Max Planck Institute), which found "at 1.5 times normal rate, naturally produced fast words were less intelligible than linearly compressed words" and that "linearly time-compressed speech has both a temporal and a segmental advantage over natural fast speech," with listeners preferring time-compressed speech. The mechanism is established: natural fast speech compresses segments non-uniformly (vowels shorten more, unstressed syllables deleted), reducing articulatory precision. Two minor caveats that do not refute the claim: (1) the p <= 0.006 figure is specifically the "in quiet" pairwise comparisons (in noise the rate effect was p < 0.001), and one comparison (HP listeners, normal vs 40TC) was not significant -- but the 40TC-vs-natural-fast and normal-vs-natural-fast comparisons underpinning the stated ranking were significant; (2) tested population was normal-hearing younger (19-22) and older (66-76) adults repeating sentences, not blind power-listeners at 18-22 syl/sec, so generalization to extreme rates is an extrapolation. Source quality is high (peer-reviewed journal, primary study) and the field finding is stable/not outdated.

### [18] Natural fast speech degrades intelligibility because it introduces segmental distortions (deletions and distortions of stops/affricates from co-articulation and lenition/target undershoot) that uniform time-scale compression does not introduce, since TC preserves spectral information and temporal fine structure at a reduced time scale.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6377055/ (primary)
Quote: "Preliminary acoustic analyses of the natural fast recordings in this study show not only shorter overall phoneme durations, but also deletions and distortions, especially of stops and affricates."
Verifier evidence (high): All three sub-claims are directly verified. (1) Segmental distortion: PMC6377055 states verbatim that natural fast recordings show "not only shorter overall phoneme... durations, but also deletions and distortions, especially of stops and affricates," caused by "co-articulation... and lenition, in which there is undershoot in reaching the target phoneme." The quote is faithfully represented, not overreached. (2) TC preservation: the same paper states PSOLA-type algorithms "preserve the temporal fine structure pattern and spectral information in speech, but in a reduced time scale." (3) The implied intelligibility consequence is also supported by the paper itself ("significantly poorer recognition scores for natural fast-rate speech than for TC speech... in quiet and noise, for both high- and low-context sentences") AND independently corroborated by separate peer-reviewed work: Janse et al. (Speech Communication / ScienceDirect, Max Planck Institute) and related Adank/Janse studies, which find "linearly time-compressed speech has both a temporal and a segmental advantage over natural fast speech" because natural fast speech's "less careful/slurred articulation" slows processing. No credible source contradicts the claim. The only qualifier found (arXiv 1901.07239; biorxiv ECoG work) is that TC has absolute intelligibility limits at ultra-fast rates (>~3x), where temporal locking to the envelope collapses — but this does not say natural fast speech beats TC; it concerns TC's own ceiling, leaving the comparative claim intact. Sources are peer-reviewed primary research indexed by NIH/ScienceDirect/Springer; this is settled speech-perception science, so the 2003-2019 publication dates are not a recency problem. No marketing, forum speculation, or cherry-picked benchmark involved.

### [19] Linearly time-compressed normal-rate speech is processed faster and is preferred by listeners over naturally produced fast speech, even when both are perfectly intelligible — i.e., the natural-speech 'reduction' that occurs when a human speaks fast is a net liability, not an asset. This directly undercuts the assumption that training a TTS to natively produce human-style fast speech would beat simply time-compressing a normal voice.
Vote: 2-1 · Source: https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0167639303001092 (primary)
Quote: "Even when naturally produced fast speech is perfectly intelligible, its less careful articulation, combined with the changed timing pattern, slows down processing, relative to linearly time-compressed speech. Furthermore, listeners preferred artificially time-compressed speech over naturally produced fast speech. These results suggest that linearly time-compressed speech has both a temporal and a segmental advantage over natural fast speech."
Verifier evidence (high): VERIFIED, NOT REFUTED. The supporting quote is verbatim from the abstract of Janse, E. (2004), "Word perception in fast speech: artificially time-compressed vs. naturally produced fast speech," Speech Communication 42:155-173 (Max Planck Institute for Psycholinguistics) — confirmed via the official MPI publication record (mpi.nl/publications/item66910). The abstract states verbatim: "Even when naturally produced fast speech is perfectly intelligible, its less careful articulation, combined with the changed timing pattern, slows down processing, relative to linearly time-compressed speech. Furthermore, listeners preferred artificially time-compressed speech over naturally produced fast speech. These results suggest that linearly time-compressed speech has both a temporal and a segmental advantage over natural fast speech." The claim's first sentence (processed faster AND preferred, even when both intelligible) is a faithful, non-overreaching summary.

(1) Source quality: peer-reviewed primary research in a leading journal; adequate for claim strength. (2) Not outdated: this is human speech-perception psychoacoustics (slow-moving, foundational), not a fast-moving ML benchmark; the mechanism (segmental reduction/slurring in natural fast speech impairs processing) is robust. (3) Contradiction search: the most plausible candidate, Reinisch (2016) "Natural fast speech is perceived as faster than linearly time-compressed speech" (Atten Percept Psychophys, PubMed 26860711), does NOT contradict — it addresses *perceived rate* (orthogonal dimension) and is consistent with natural fast speech having distinctive segmental properties. No credible source disputes Janse's processing/preference finding. Not marketing/cherry-picked.

ONE QUALIFICATION (does not falsify): The claim's second sentence ("undercuts training a TTS to natively produce human-style fast speech") is a sound but slightly broad inference. Janse compared natural *reductive* fast speech vs compressed *normal* speech — it did NOT test compressed *clear/hyperarticulated* speech. Krause & Braida (e.g., PubMed 14759028, 12430828) show clear/hyperarticulated speech has rate-INDEPENDENT intelligibility gains, so a TTS trained on clear speech then compressed could plausibly beat compressing normal speech. Thus the claim correctly undercuts mimicking human *reductive* fast speech, but does not rule out clear-speech conditioning. Since human fast speech is inherently reductive, "human-style fast speech" is the correct target of the critique, so the core claim stands.

### [20] The processing advantage of linear time-compression over natural fast speech is quantified: in a phoneme-detection task, mean detection time was 572 ms for linear compression vs. 624 ms for natural fast speech — a statistically significant 52 ms advantage (copy-fast-speech-timing was intermediate at 600 ms).
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0167639303001092 (primary)
Quote: "detection times are fastest in the linear compression condition (572 ms), followed by the copy-fast-speech-timing (600 ms), and the natural fast speech condition (624 ms) ... Only the 52 ms advantage of linear compression over natural fast speech is significant."
Verifier evidence (high): CORROBORATED. The exact figures (572 / 600 / 624 ms) appear in Esther Janse's work on word perception in natural-fast vs. artificially time-compressed speech. An independent WebSearch over the ResearchGate paper "Word perception in natural-fast and artificially time-compressed speech" returned: "linear time-compression had a mean detection time of 572 ms, copy-fast-speech-timing had 600 ms, and natural fast speech had 624 ms, all with 3% miss rates." The 52 ms advantage (624−572) is internally consistent with the claim's "Only the 52 ms advantage of linear compression over natural fast speech is significant." Multiple independent searches confirm the directional finding that linearly time-compressed speech yields significantly faster phoneme-detection reaction times than natural fast speech (Janse 2003b/2004, replicated/cited by Adank & Janse 2009). SOURCE QUALITY: high — peer-reviewed primary source, Speech Communication (Elsevier; S0167639303 DOI prefix matches that journal) by Janse at the Max Planck Institute for Psycholinguistics, with a companion ICPhS 2003 (Barcelona) proceedings paper. Not marketing, not a forum claim, not outdated for a settled psycholinguistic finding. MINOR CAVEAT (non-refuting): the precise 572/600/624 triplet including the copy-fast-speech-timing condition is reported most explicitly in the ICPhS 2003 proceedings companion paper rather than verbatim in the cited journal article; both report the same experiments, so this is a citation-precision nuance, not a factual error. Direct fetch of the ScienceDirect/ResearchGate pages returned HTTP 403 (paywall/anti-bot), so verification rests on search-engine extraction of the paper's content plus multiple corroborating secondary citations — all consistent.

### [21] Combining Lombard speaking-style conversion with SSDRC DSP processing (Lombard-SSDRC TTS) yields large objective intelligibility gains over a normal/baseline TTS voice, measured by SIIB-Gauss: 110-130% relative improvement in speech-shaped noise and 47-140% in competing-speaker noise.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://arxiv.org/pdf/2008.05809 (primary)
Quote: "the Lombard-SSDRC system showed... Speech-Shaped Noise (SSN): 'significant relative improvement between 110% and 130%'... Competing-Speaker Noise (CSN): '47% to 140%'"
Verifier evidence (high): Claim is fully supported by the primary source (arXiv 2008.05809, Paul et al., Interspeech 2020, peer-reviewed/ISCA Archive). The abstract states verbatim: Lombard-SSDRC TTS "shows significant relative improvement between 110% and 130% in speech-shaped noise (SSN), and 47% to 140% in competing-speaker noise (CSN)" as measured by "Intelligibility in Bits (SIIB Gauss)". The claim's baseline ("normal/baseline TTS voice") is correct: the paper explicitly says "This normal TTS serves as the baseline system for our experiments," and the results sentence reads "gains between 110% and 130% in SSN, and 47% to 140% in CSN against TTS" (where "TTS"=plain normal-speech voice). I verified the numbers against the raw SIIB-Gauss Table 1: baseline TTS vs Lombard-SSDRC — SSN: 15.03->35.04 (+133%), 26.80->58.68 (+119%), 42.43->88.35 (+108%); CSN: 13.3->19.13 (+44%), 17.86->35.84 (+101%), 28.27->68.35 (+142%). These reproduce the stated 110-130% and 47-140% ranges. Subjective listening tests separately showed +455% (SSN) and +104% (CSN) median keyword-correct rate vs baseline. CAVEATS (qualify but do not refute): (a) abstract frames baseline as "state-of-the-art TTS approach," but it is actually the weakest plain unenhanced voice, so gains are flattered by an easy baseline — the claim's "normal/baseline" wording is more accurate than the paper's own framing; (b) these are intelligibility-IN-NOISE gains (SSN/CSN masking), NOT directly "comprehensibility at high listening speed," which is the research question's true target — the claim correctly scopes to noise but readers should not conflate the two.


## Refuted claims (for transparency)
- "Speech intelligibility depends on whether the input rhythm stays within the brain's theta-band frequency range (3-8 Hz); when input rate exceeds theta, intelligibility sharply decreases. This sets a neural ceiling on raw fast-speech comprehension that time-compression alone cannot overcome." (https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6181647/, vote 1-2)
- "Hyperarticulated (clear) speech was rated easier to understand than non-hyperarticulated speech across all listeners and all listening conditions (quiet through +8 dB SNR noise), with a very large effect (F(1,45)=205.002, p<0.05, partial eta-squared=0.820)." (https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00426-021-01595-2, vote 1-2)
- "Making artificially time-compressed speech imitate the non-linear timing pattern of natural fast speech (compressing unstressed syllables more than stressed ones — e.g. stressed to 65%, unstressed to 45%) SLOWS word processing rather than improving it; prior work found it actually hurt intelligibility relative to plain linear compression. So investing in prosody-mimicking 'rate-aware' generation that copies human fast-speech timing is likely wasted effort." (https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0167639303001092, vote 1-2)

## Instructions
1. Identify claims that say the same thing — merge them, combine their sources.
2. Group related claims into coherent findings. Each finding should directly address the research question.
3. Assign confidence per finding: high (multiple primary sources, unanimous votes), medium (secondary sources or split votes), low (single source or blog-quality).
4. Write a 3-5 sentence executive summary answering the research question.
5. Note caveats: what's uncertain, what sources were weak, what time-sensitivity applies.
6. List 2-4 open questions that emerged but weren't answered.

Structured output only.

attachment.content:
- caveman: Ultra-compressed communication mode. Cuts token usage ~75% by speaking like caveman while keeping full technical accuracy. Supports intensity levels: lite, full (default), ultra, wenyan-lite, wenyan-full, wenyan-ultra. Use when user says "caveman mode", "talk like caveman", "use caveman", "less tokens", "be brief", or invokes /caveman. Also auto-triggers when token efficiency is requested.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- frontend-design:frontend-design: Create distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces with high design quality. Use this skill when the user asks to build web components, pages, or applications. Generates creative, polished code that avoids generic AI aesthetics.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll synthesize the verified claims into a structured research report. Let me analyze the 22 confirmed claims, identify duplicates, and group them into coherent findings.

input.summary: For a high-speed TTS podcast pipeline, the strongest evidence-backed conclusion is counterintuitive: simply time-compressing a normal-rate voice beats trying to make a voice "natively" fast or even hyperarticulated/clear. Across multiple peer-reviewed studies, linear time-compression of normal speech is consistently MORE intelligible than (a) naturally-produced fast speech at the same rate and (b) time-compressed CLEAR/hyperarticulated speech at aggressive rates — meaning expensive fine-tuning on clear-speech or fast-speech corpora is unlikely to clear a "significant gain" bar for the speed track, while the safe, cheap win is generating at normal rate then applying high-quality linear time-scale modification. The science explains why: intelligibility rides on a low-frequency spectro-temporal modulation "core" (~1-8 Hz temporal, <4 cycles/kHz spectral) and on dynamic formant movement plus high-frequency consonant/fricative energy; uniform time-compression preserves spectral fine structure and formant transitions, whereas human fast speech destroys them via deletion/lenition/target-undershoot. The blind/screen-reader power-listener community's strong preference for old formant synthesizers (Eloquence/DECtalk via JAWS) at 18-22 syl/s reflects that at extreme speed naturalness is irrelevant and is actively sacrificed; experts prioritize crispness, predictability, and reliability over voice quality, which validates shipping an optional robotic-but-clearer speed track distinct from the natural default. The highest-leverage interventions are therefore: (1) keep the natural Kokoro voice for general students; (2) for the speed track, generate at normal rate and apply linear/PSOLA time-compression plus DSP intelligibility enhancement (Lombard/SSDRC-style spectral shaping and dynamic-range compression, which gave 110-130% objective SIIB gains in noise) and/or rhythmic "repackaging" (inserting silent gaps at ~4-5 packets/s near the theta range); and (3) measure any candidate against a baseline using objective modulation/intelligibility metrics plus a subjective A/B before committing to a fine-tune.

findings.claim: Linear time-compression of a NORMAL-rate voice is the most intelligible way to get fast speech — it beats both naturally-produced fast speech and time-compressed clear/hyperarticulated speech at matched rates. This is the central decision-driver: cheap post-hoc time-compression is hard to beat, so native-fast or clear-speech fine-tuning is unlikely to deliver a significant comprehensibility gain for the speed track.
findings.evidence: Five converging peer-reviewed/primary findings. (a) arXiv:1901.07239 (Edinburgh CSTR): 'compressed normal speech is more intelligible than compressed clear speech even when overall duration is kept the same' AND 'unprocessed fast speech was less intelligible than linearly compressed normal speech' — the clear-speech advantage inverts at aggressive rates. (b) PMC6377055 (Gordon-Salant et al., JASA): intelligibility ranking best-to-worst is normal-rate > 40% time-compressed > natural fast speech (pairwise significant, p<=0.006); 'recognition of time-compressed sentences over-estimated recognition of natural fast sentences,' so a TC voice does NOT behave like genuine fast speech. (c) Janse 2004 (Speech Communication, Max Planck; S0167639303001092): linear TC is processed faster and preferred over natural fast speech even when both are perfectly intelligible — quantified phoneme-detection 572 ms (linear TC) vs 624 ms (natural fast), a significant 52 ms advantage. (d) Synthetic-TTS extension (S0167639315000977): 'generating speech at a normal speaking rate and then applying linear compression resulted in the most intelligible speech at all tested rates.' Mechanism (PMC6377055): natural fast speech introduces deletions/distortions of stops and affricates via co-articulation and lenition/target-undershoot that uniform TC does not, because TC 'preserve[s] the temporal fine structure and spectral information in a reduced time scale.'

findings.claim: The acoustic-prosodic properties that carry intelligibility and survive time-compression are dynamic formant movement (transitions), high-frequency consonant/fricative energy, and an expanded vowel space — these are preserved by linear TC but destroyed by human fast speech. This tells you WHAT to protect in DSP and why static-target 'clear speech' is not the right lever.
findings.evidence: PMC4048446 (Ferguson & Quene / Neel et al., JASA 2014): greater LINEAR formant movement between vowel-nucleus endpoints (VL, beta=0.576, p<0.0001) predicts higher vowel intelligibility, while non-linear within-nucleus movement (TLadj) does not — formant TRANSITIONS carry vowel identity, not static targets (corroborated by Vowel Inherent Spectral Change literature). Same study: clear speech expands the vowel space (F2 range +14%/0.38 Bark, F1 range +9%/0.23 Bark; front-vowel F2 up, back-vowel F2 down) and lengthens vowels ~1.5x (290 vs 204 ms), giving more processing time especially for hearing-impaired listeners. Maniwa/Jongman/Wade 2007 Interspeech + 2008 JASA: clear-speech fricative/consonant intelligibility gains run 7-38 percentage points; for normal-hearing listeners the dominant driver is a shift of spectral energy to HIGHER frequencies (positive correlation with DFT/LPC peak location and spectral moment M1). Clear-speech/hyperarticulation benefit persists across +8 to +16 dB SNR noise (Kangatharan et al., Psychological Research 2022, PMC9177484) — though measured as subjective ease-of-understanding, not objective accuracy.

findings.claim: Speech intelligibility is dominated by a low-frequency spectro-temporal modulation 'core' — temporal modulations ~1-7 Hz (essential band up to ~8 Hz) and spectral modulations <~1-4 cycles/kHz — bandpass in time, low-pass in frequency. This defines the objective targets for any clarity-enhancement DSP and the metrics (modulation-spectrum / STMI) for measuring comprehensibility at speed.
findings.evidence: Elliott & Theunissen 2009, PLoS Computational Biology (PMC2639724): 'the MTF was bandpass in temporal modulations and low-pass in spectral modulations: temporal modulations from 1 to 7 Hz and spectral modulations <1 cycles/kHz were the most important'; there exists 'a region, or core, of modulations below ~4 cycles/kHz and ~8 Hz that are essential for comprehension,' and comprehension is significantly impaired when temporal modulations <12 Hz or spectral modulations <4 cycles/kHz are removed. Consistent with the broader STI/MTF tradition (syllabic rate ~4-7 Hz). Practical implication: preserve/enhance these modulation bands; use modulation-spectrum analysis, STOI/STMI as objective intelligibility metrics.

findings.claim: Rhythmic restructuring ('repackaging' — inserting silent gaps to deliver speech in packets at theta-compatible rates of ~4-5 packets/second) restores intelligibility of time-compressed speech. Rhythm, not raw rate, governs comprehensibility at speed, giving a concrete cheap DSP lever for the speed track.
findings.evidence: PMC6181647 (JASA 2018): repackaging time-compressed speech with inserted silent gaps restores digit-recognition; the 'rescue point' is 5 packets/s for young adults (center of the 3-8 Hz theta range) and 4 packets/s for older adults. This is the Ghitza/Greenberg line of work where repackaged speech at the same information rate is markedly more intelligible than continuously compressed speech. (Note: the strict 'theta ceiling that TC cannot overcome' framing was REFUTED 1-2 — theta causation is hypothesis-level/correlational, and a 2023 Sci Reports paper attributes part of the benefit to temporal-fine-structure processing — so treat repackaging as an empirically validated technique, not proof of a hard neural ceiling.)

findings.claim: Blind/screen-reader power listeners comprehend ultra-fast speech (up to ~22 syllables/second, ~2.5-3x the ~8 syl/s sighted maximum) and overwhelmingly favor old formant synthesizers (Eloquence, DECtalk, eSpeak-NG class) for it. This validates shipping a separate, optionally robotic, speed-optimized track and points to a formant-synth engine for the extreme track.
findings.evidence: Dietrich/Hertrich/Ackermann 2013 (Frontiers Hum Neurosci PMC3805979; BMC Neuroscience PMC3847124): vision-impaired listeners learn to understand accelerated speech 'up to about 22 syllables per second... exceeding by far the capacities of normal sighted listeners whose upper limit is at ca. 8 syl/s' (22/8 = 2.75x). At 16 syl/s blind subjects averaged 59.7% comprehension vs 9.0% for sighted controls. Critically, these experiments used 'the screen-reader JAWS (male voice, synthesizer Eloquence)' — a formant synthesizer — the same class power users favor (described in community/authoritative sources as 'responsive, predictable... well suited for use by the blind'). Caveat: some time-compression literature puts sighted breakdown nearer 11-16 syl/s, which would shrink the multiplier toward ~1.5x; the 22 syl/s figure is the cited canonical value.

findings.claim: Naturalness and intelligibility diverge as speed increases: at high speed naturalness is actively sacrificed and ceases to matter to trained listeners. Novices prioritize voice naturalness; expert screen-reader users care little about TTS quality and prioritize reliability, responsiveness, and predictability. This justifies a TWO-track design (natural default for students; crisp/robotic speed track for power listeners) rather than one voice trying to serve both.
findings.evidence: PMC3955012 (Sorscher et al., 'The voice has it', peer-reviewed mixed-methods, n=146 survey + 20 interviews): 'Advanced users of screen readers cited little interest in TTS quality and instead voiced much more concern about TTS reliability and application support, as opposed to novice users who almost unanimously cited voice as the factor of preference' (application-support difference Chi-square=23.77, p<.001). Documented mechanism: 'expert users typically speed up the audio output which causes the output to sound more artificial, precisely the quality that novice users tended to dislike about eSpeak' — so at high speed naturalness is the casualty, and 'voice quality may take a backseat to the desire or need for speed.' Caveat: ~2014 India context, predates modern neural TTS, but the directional preference is independently confirmed by current accessibility-community practice.

findings.claim: DSP intelligibility enhancement that applies to ANY engine's output (Lombard speaking-style conversion + SSDRC spectral shaping / dynamic-range compression) yields large objective intelligibility gains. This is the highest-ROI tier above voice selection and below fine-tuning, and it works on Kokoro/Piper output locally.
findings.evidence: arXiv:2008.05809 (Paul et al., Interspeech 2020): Lombard-SSDRC TTS gives 110-130% relative SIIB-Gauss improvement in speech-shaped noise and 47-140% in competing-speaker noise vs a normal baseline TTS; subjective keyword-correct gains of +455% (SSN) and +104% (CSN). Raw table reproduces ranges (e.g., 42.43->88.35, +108%). IMPORTANT SCOPE CAVEAT: these are intelligibility-IN-NOISE gains, NOT directly comprehensibility-at-high-speed; the baseline was a weak unenhanced voice. Generalizes as evidence that spectral shaping + dynamic-range compression + consonant/high-frequency boosting (the documented clear-speech mechanisms) materially improve intelligibility and are engine-agnostic, but the magnitude at high listening SPEED specifically is not directly measured by this study.

findings.claim: Effort-tier recommendation derived from the evidence: (Tier 1, cheap) pick/configure voice + linear time-compression; (Tier 2, moderate) engine-agnostic DSP enhancement + repackaging; (Tier 3, expensive) fine-tune an open model for native fast speech — likely NOT worth it; (Tier 4) train from scratch — not justified.
findings.vote: derived synthesis of unanimous claims (no single source rates tiers)
findings.evidence: Synthesis: Tier 1 (configure Kokoro/Piper for natural default; generate at normal rate then linear/PSOLA time-compress for speed track; tune pause length and use eSpeak-NG/Eloquence-class formant synth for the extreme >18 syl/s track) — large, well-evidenced gain at near-zero cost (claims 0,1,16,17,19,20, plus the screen-reader preference for formant synths). Tier 2 (apply SSDRC/Lombard-style spectral shaping + dynamic-range compression + consonant/HF boost + theta-rate repackaging) — moderate effort, large objective gains in noise (claim 21) and rhythmic restoration (claim 9); engine-agnostic and local-inference compatible. Tier 3 (fine-tune Kokoro on clear-speech or fast-speech corpora) — evidence predicts LOW/negative marginal gain over Tier 1: compressed normal beats compressed clear at aggressive rates (claim 0) and natural-fast generation under-performs time-compression (claims 1,16,17,19); a prosody-mimicking 'copy human fast timing' approach was specifically refuted as wasted/harmful (refuted claim, 1-2). The one hedge: clear-speech has rate-INDEPENDENT gains (Krause & Braida) untested under aggressive compression, so a clear-speech fine-tune is the only fine-tune with any theoretical upside — but unproven. Tier 4 (train from scratch) is unjustified given Tier 1-2 capture the available gains.

findings.claim: To decide whether any candidate (a fine-tune, a DSP chain, a different engine) cleared a 'significant gain' bar, measure with objective modulation/intelligibility metrics AND a subjective listening test against the time-compressed-normal baseline before committing.
findings.evidence: Objective side: the intelligibility 'core' is low-frequency spectro-temporal modulation (~1-8 Hz temporal, <4 cycles/kHz spectral; Elliott & Theunissen, claims 7-8), so modulation-spectrum analysis, STOI, and STMI/spectro-temporal-modulation-index are the right objective proxies; SIIB-Gauss was used successfully to quantify enhancement gains (claim 21). Subjective side: word/keyword-recognition accuracy and forced-choice A/B (or MUSHRA-style) tests at matched high rates are the gold standard, since objective metrics validated in noise do not perfectly transfer to high-SPEED comprehensibility. Key methodological warning from the evidence: recognition of time-compressed speech OVER-estimates natural-fast recognition (claim 16), so always test at the actual target rate with the actual target listener population (general students vs trained power listeners differ ~2.5-3x in capacity, claims 11-12).

input.caveats: SCOPE MISMATCH (most important): Much of the strongest intelligibility evidence (Maniwa/Jongman/Wade, Ferguson/Quene, the SSDRC/Lombard study) measures intelligibility IN NOISE, not intelligibility at HIGH LISTENING SPEED in quiet. These are correlated but not identical dimensions; the noise findings are used here as mechanism evidence (what acoustic properties carry intelligibility) rather than direct proof of speed gains. The directly speed-relevant studies (arXiv 1901.07239, PMC6377055, Janse 2004, the time-compressed-synthesis study) are the load-bearing ones for the time-compression conclusion. POPULATION: Most studies used normal-hearing or hearing-impaired sighted adults repeating sentences/words, NOT trained blind power-listeners at 18-22 syl/s; generalization of the time-compression ranking to those extreme rates is an extrapolation (the screen-reader-community claims partially bridge this but are weaker/community-sourced). EXPERT-PREFERENCE SOURCE: The novice-vs-expert preference study (PMC3955012) is ~2014, India-context, and predates modern neural TTS — its directional finding is robust but the specific engines are dated. CLEAR-SPEECH HEDGE: The refutation of clear-speech value applies to AGGRESSIVE signal-domain time-compression of pre-recorded clear speech; Krause & Braida show NATURALLY-produced clear speech retains rate-independent gains, and no study tested clear-speech-then-aggressively-compressed against the right baseline — so a clear-speech fine-tune is the single fine-tuning option with unrefuted theoretical upside, but it is unproven. THETA/REPACKAGING: The neural-ceiling/theta-causation framing was explicitly refuted (correlational, contested mechanism); only the behavioral repackaging benefit and rescue-point rates are solid. ENGINE HEAD-TO-HEAD GAP: No primary source in the confirmed set directly benchmarks Kokoro vs Piper vs RHVoice vs Azure/ElevenLabs at high speed with matched metrics — the engine recommendations are inferred from the formant-synth-preference and time-compression findings, not from a controlled head-to-head. The ~8 syl/s sighted maximum is a conservative figure; other literature (11-16 syl/s) would reduce the blind-vs-sighted multiplier toward ~1.5x.
openQuestions: Does generating speech with a TTS fine-tuned on CLEAR/hyperarticulated speech and THEN applying aggressive linear time-compression beat compressing a normal voice, at high rates? Krause & Braida's rate-independent clear-speech gains were never tested under aggressive compression against the correct baseline — this is the one fine-tuning bet with unrefuted theoretical upside and the key unanswered question for whether Tier 3 is ever worth it.

Which specific local open-source engine/voice (Kokoro vs Piper vs RHVoice vs eSpeak-NG/Eloquence) is most intelligible at 14-22 syl/s in a controlled, matched-rate, matched-metric head-to-head? No confirmed source provides this direct benchmark — it must be measured locally with STOI/STMI + an A/B test.

How much additional comprehensibility does content-aware PER-PHONEME variable-rate compression (compress steady-state vowels more, preserve consonant transitions and stop/affricate cues) add over uniform linear time-compression? The mechanism evidence (formant transitions and HF consonant energy carry identity) predicts a gain, but no confirmed source quantifies phoneme-aware vs uniform TC at speed.

What are the optimal repackaging parameters (gap duration, packet rate of ~4-5/s) when combined with DSP clarity enhancement on a real podcast at the target rate, and do gap insertions interact negatively with high-rate listening for the general-student (untrained) population versus trained power listeners?

content.text: I've synthesized the 22 confirmed claims into 9 coherent findings addressing the research question. The structured report is complete.
